test_text.title: Synmouth Museum's Children's Art and Craft Workshops

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a woman calling a local museum about children's art and craft workshops.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Synmouth Museum.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm interested in the children's workshops, and I'd like a little more information please.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you mean the art and craft workshops?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, a friend of a friend mentioned them.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The children do painting and make models and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Where to begin?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: First of all, as you probably know, they run every Saturday.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The workshops are organized every Saturday, so Saturday has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Synmouth Museum.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm interested in the children's workshops, and I'd like a little more information please.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you mean the art and craft workshops?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, a friend of a friend mentioned them.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The children do painting and make models and so forth.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Where to begin?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: First of all, as you probably know, they run every Saturday.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine, and what about ages?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, all ages from 5 upwards are welcome, though we do ask that children below 8 years of age are accompanied by an adult.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Fine, that wouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What about cost?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I think you'll find them very reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's £2.50 a child, with 80 pence off for two or more children from the same family.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, very reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And are they held in the main museum?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Not exactly, they're nearby.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you give me the full address?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I don't know the area very well.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Winter House.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that's in Tamer Street.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you spell that please?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, TAMER Street.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And I do need to tell you that there's a security entrance, so you need to press the green button for someone to let you in.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Don't press the red button please, but don't worry, it's all clearly labeled.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, and one more question, is parking available nearby?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We're driving in from out of town.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Your best bet is to leave your car at the back of the library, on a Saturday morning, there are plenty of spaces there.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's right next door to the museum.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And can I ask about booking places?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, and I must tell you, you really should book by calling the education department here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm sorry.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Should I have rung them instead of the main museum number?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: No, that's fine this time.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Please don't worry.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But for future reference, I'll give you the direct number.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Great, I've got that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: But I'm very happy to give you information about the next two workshops.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: On Saturday the 16th, there's Building Castles.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sounds great.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: This involves quite a bit of glue, so just make sure the kids are in old clothes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I know, ones I don't mind getting mucky.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Exactly, and if possible, could you bring along bottle tops which the children might be able to use in the models, you know as decoration?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We'll certainly try to find some for you.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Then the following week, that'll be the 23rd, won't it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: On that day, it's what we call Undersea Worlds.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. This is where they make scenes with fishes, underground caverns, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that likely to get very dirty?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Lots of paint splashes?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Not really, so we don't recommend any special clothes for that one.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But if you could search out some silver paper to bring along to use in the sessions, you know, it's shiny, it looks like water, that would be great.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We'll see what we can come up with.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, thank you ever so much for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The sessions sound really good, and I'll certainly book up for the next two.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Lovely, thanks very much for ringing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Bye.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Bye bye.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: CHILDREN'S ART AND CRAFT WORKSHOPS

test_text.title: Train Services and Local Attractions in Trebirch

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear a customer services official giving information to a passenger at a railway station in a town called treborth.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, um, my family and I are staying here in Trebirch for a week or two, and we wanted to know about the train services.
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: We're hoping to do a few local trips.
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Well, I can give you lots of details about all the trains going from Trebirch in the southwest, uh, this leaflet will be very helpful, but I can tell you some of the main things.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: We've got 2 main train stations in the town.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: King Street is for local commuter lines and regional services.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: W- what about trains to London?
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: I'll need to go there on business for one day.
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: Then you need to go to Central Station.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: That's for all the national services.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: There are regular trains to London.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: They leave Trebirch every half hour on weekdays, and every hour at weekends.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: It takes about 2 hours, a bit longer on Sundays.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: You've got a choice of 1st and 2nd class, and there's a buffet car, though refreshments are included in the cost of a first class ticket.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Ah. Right.
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: Um. And have you got any information on different ticket types?
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: Yes, there's a range of ticket prices depending on when you travel and when you buy your ticket.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: There's a standard open ticket, which doesn't have any restrictions.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: This can be bought in advance or on the day.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: You can also get various discounted tickets.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: A popular one is called the Supersave, and this is OK for travel after 8:45.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Then that is the Special ticket, which is valid for travel after 10:15. The Special tickets are also valid for travel at weekends.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Uh. The cheapest tickets are called Advance, and you have to buy them at least 6 days ahead.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Only a certain number are available, and you have to make seat reservations for these.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thanks, and are there lots of places to go to around here?
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, you can enjoy many days out.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Um. There's the Merthyr Mining Museum, which is only half an hour from Trebirch by train. Your children will find it just as fascinating as any theme park, and they can ride in the original miners' lifts and on the coal trains.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: There are special excursion tickets which include entrance fees.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Mainline trains also offer direct services to Bristol.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Where you can visit the docks or spend a great day out with the children in the zoo, which is set in the Parkland that used to surround the old castle.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Er, special family awayday fares are available for this service now during the school holidays.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Uh. Alternatively, you can be in Birmingham in only an hour and a half, where there's lots to see and do, including the new and internationally acclaimed climbing wall.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Built on the site of the old aquarium.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: We will also be running a special service to Newport when the new Science Museum opens next year, as we anticipate a lot of visitors in the opening weeks.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: I'd advise you to call early to book your tickets.
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Is that OK?
audioscript.speaker: RAILPERSON

audioscript.text: Yes, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: CUSTOMER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: Uh. Alternatively, you can be in Birmingham in only an hour and a half, where there's lots to see and do, including the new and internationally acclaimed climbing wall.
utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: Built on the site of the old aquarium.
utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: We will also be running a special service to Newport when the new Science Museum opens next year, as we anticipate a lot of visitors in the opening weeks.
utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: I'd advise you to call early to book your tickets.
utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: Is that OK?
utterances.speaker: RAILPERSON

utterances.text: Yes, thanks.
utterances.speaker: CUSTOMER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

National services depart from the <input> railway station.
Trains for London depart every <input> each day during the week.
The price of a first class ticket includes <input>.

test_text.title: Progress and New Targets in Sandy's Dissertation Work

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a discussion between a tutor and an education student called Sandy about the students' dissertation.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Sandy.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: How have you been getting on with your dissertation?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Fine, and I've been working hard on the various action points we agreed on our last tutorial.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Do you want to talk me through what you've done?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, sure.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Well, we agreed on three main targets for me to aim for.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: The first one was to find out about suitable data analysis software, yes.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: And what I decided to do was to look through catalogs specializing in it.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: That's a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What did you come up with?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I found the names of two promising ones.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: But I also thought it would be worthwhile talking to a lecturer.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Who did you see?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Jane Prince.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Do you know her?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: She's in the computer center.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: She's the new head.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, well she was very helpful.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Oh, that's good.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Did she suggest anything in particular?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, she recommended software called Vivat, and said I should book up for a couple of practice sessions using Vivat.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Great, I'm sure you'll find them useful.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And of course, the second target was to draw up a survey checklist, which I...
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Yes, you emailed me it last week.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Have you had a chance to look?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Um. I think it's good, uh, very much on the right lines.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I'd say your first two sections are spot on.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I wouldn't suggest that you change anything there, but in section 3, you really do need to have questions on teaching experience.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah, I was thinking that section looked a bit short.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And my third target was to do further reading on discipline.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I mentioned a couple of writers, didn't I?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Well, I got hold of the Banerjee, and I thought that was excellent. But I'm afraid I didn't manage to get hold of the essays about classroom management, you know, the ones by Simon Ericsson. The bookshop said it was out of print, and the library doesn't have a copy.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And I'm afraid I've lent my copy to another student.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What I suggest you do is try the library again.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: This time apply for it through the service called special loans.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Have you done that before?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You're entitled to 6 books a year.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, no problem.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: That's what I'll do.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: So, lots of useful work done.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So let's look at some new targets.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: We'll start by having a chat about your chapter 1.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I very much enjoyed reading it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Your written style is very clear, and you've included lots of interesting descriptions of education in your target area.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I've just got a couple of suggestions for some additional work.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Could I just ask what do you think I should call it?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Well, I'd go for something like context review.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What do you think?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well, short and to the point.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Now as regards specific areas to work on, I'd be quite interested to have a few more statistics about the schools in the different zones.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, that wouldn't be a problem.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: I can get them from the internet.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And although you did make a reference to quite a few different writers, I think you should aim to cite more works written later than 2000.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, that's more difficult, but I can try.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: When do you want that done by?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Oh, it's not urgent.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Um. I should aim for the end of term. But in the meantime, I think you should also be thinking about Chapter 2.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Should I be drafting it already?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: No, but I think you should note down its main sections.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: You know, I always find that the hardest part.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Yeah, I always find it helpful to put some ideas on index cards.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: Um, and then you can sort them, and even lay them out on the floor.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It's a real help.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Well.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: I'll certainly try it.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: When would the deadline be for that?
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: My advice would be to get it done before you embark upon the research.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh. You can always change it later if you need to.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK, I'll get going on that then.
audioscript.speaker: SANDY

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: The Evolution of Moving Pictures from Horse Racing Photos to Talkies

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear part of a lecture about the history of moving pictures.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Many believe that the story first began in America in 1877, when two friends were arguing over whether a horse ever had all four feet or hooves off the ground when it galloped.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To settle the bet, a photographer was asked to photograph a horse galloping, and the bet was settled, because you could see that all the hooves were off the ground in some of the photos.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What was even more interesting was that if the photos were shown in quick succession, the horse looked like it was running, in other words, moving pictures.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The person who became interested in taking the moving pictures to its next step was the famous American inventor Thomas Edison.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Actually, he didn't do the work himself, but rather asked a young Scotsman in his employ to design a system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which he did.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, this young fellow was clever, because the first thing he did was study other systems primitive as they were of moving pictures, and then put all the existing technologies together to make the first entire motion picture system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He designed a camera, a projection device, and the film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The system was first shown in New York in 1894 and was really very popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Apparently, people lined up around the block to see the wonderful new invention.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There were however a couple of problems with the system.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The camera weighed over 200 kilograms, and only one person at a time could see the film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well now, news of the new system in America traveled fast, and a number of rival European systems started to appear once people had heard about it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The single problem with all the systems was they couldn't really project the film onto a screen, you know, so more than one person could see it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then in 1895, three systems were all developed, more or less at the same time, and independently of each other.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I guess the most famous of these was by the Lumiere brothers from France.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they called their system the cinematograph, which of course is where the word cinema comes from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There were also two brothers in Germany who developed a successful system, and they called it a bioskop.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, now once the problem of projection had been solved, the next challenge for the inventors was to make the films longer and more interesting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A continuing problem at the time was that the films had a tendency to break when they were being played.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A problem which was caused by the tension between the two wheels or reels as they are called, which hold the film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, this problem was solved by two American brothers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They developed the Lantham Loop, which was the simple addition of a third reel between the two main reels, and this took all the tension away with the result that the film stopped snapping.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So now there was a real possibility of having films of more than two or three minutes, and this led to the making of The Great Train Robbery, the very first movie made.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It only lasted 11 minutes, but was an absolute sensation, and there were cases of people watching the movie and actually fainting when the character fired a gun at the camera.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Almost overnight, movies became a craze, and by 1905 people in America were lining up to see movies in store theaters, as they were called then.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I guess the next big step in terms of development of technology was to have people actually talking on the film, and the first step towards this was in 1926 when sound effects were first used on a film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't until the following year, however, that the first talkie, as they were called then, was made.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This film featured actors speaking only during parts of the film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And was called The Jazz Singer, and it wasn't until 1928 that the first all talking film was produced, and this was called The Lights of New York.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unfortunately, the sound on this early film was not very good, and I believe they put subtitles on the film.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is, they printed the dialogue along the bottom of the film to compensate for this poor sound quality.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, with the addition of sound, moving pictures became far more difficult to make.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The camera weighed over 200 kilograms, and only one person at a time could see the film.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well now, news of the new system in America traveled fast, and a number of rival European systems started to appear once people had heard about it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The single problem with all the systems was they couldn't really project the film onto a screen, you know, so more than one person could see it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then in 1895, three systems were all developed, more or less at the same time, and independently of each other.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I guess the most famous of these was by the Lumiere brothers from France.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they called their system the cinematograph, which of course is where the word cinema comes from.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There were also two brothers in Germany who developed a successful system, and they called it a bioskop.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, now once the problem of projection had been solved, the next challenge for the inventors was to make the films longer and more interesting.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A continuing problem at the time was that the films had a tendency to break when they were being played.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A problem which was caused by the tension between the two wheels or reels as they are called, which hold the film.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, this problem was solved by two American brothers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They developed the Lantham Loop, which was the simple addition of a third reel between the two main reels, and this took all the tension away with the result that the film stopped snapping.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So now there was a real possibility of having films of more than two or three minutes, and this led to the making of The Great Train Robbery, the very first movie made.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It only lasted 11 minutes, but was an absolute sensation, and there were cases of people watching the movie and actually fainting when the character fired a gun at the camera.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Almost overnight, movies became a craze, and by 1905 people in America were lining up to see movies in store theaters, as they were called then.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I guess the next big step in terms of development of technology was to have people actually talking on the film, and the first step towards this was in 1926 when sound effects were first used on a film.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It wasn't until the following year, however, that the first talkie, as they were called then, was made.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This film featured actors speaking only during parts of the film.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And was called The Jazz Singer, and it wasn't until 1928 that the first all talking film was produced, and this was called The Lights of New York.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unfortunately, the sound on this early film was not very good, and I believe they put subtitles on the film.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is, they printed the dialogue along the bottom of the film to compensate for this poor sound quality.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, with the addition of sound, moving pictures became far more difficult to make.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Some photographs of a horse running showed
options: at least one foot on the ground.

items.title: The Scotsman employed by Edison
options: designed a system to use the technology Edison had invented.
used available technology to make a new system.
was already an expert in motion picture technology.

items.title: One major problem with the first system was that
options: only one person could be filmed.
people could only see very short films.

items.title: Rival systems started to appear in Europe after people had
options: been told about the American system.

items.title: In 1895, a famous new system was developed by
options: a French and German team working together.
a German team who invented the word 'cinema'.

items.title: Longer films were not made at the time because of problems involving
The 'Lantham Loop' invention relied on
options: removing tension between the film reels.
adding three more film reels to the system.
making one of the film reels more effective.

items: The first motion picture was called The <input>.
<input> were used for the first time on film in 1926.
Subtitles were added to The Lights of New York because of its <input>.
